perturbation.original_text: During the term of the agreement and for a period of twelve (12) months thereafter, each party agrees not to solicit or recruit any employee of each other without the prior written consent of that party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of the agreement and for a period of twenty-four (24) months thereafter, each party agrees not to solicit or recruit any employee of each other without the prior written consent of that party.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the non-solicitation period to 24 months, which may be considered unreasonable and unenforceable under Florida law, depending on the specific circumstances and employee's role.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 542.335 governs the enforceability of restrictive covenants, including non-solicitation agreements.  While it permits such agreements, it requires them to be reasonable in time, area, and line of business. A 24-month non-solicitation period may be deemed unreasonable, particularly if the employee's position is not highly specialized or if the restriction is broader than necessary to protect legitimate business interests.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a 12-month non-solicitation period, while the changed text extends it to 24 months. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, this extension may be considered unreasonable under Florida Statute § 542.335, which requires restrictive covenants to be reasonable in time. Because the modified text potentially violates a key requirement (reasonableness of time) for enforceability under Florida law, a legal contradiction exists. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates the change might violate state law regarding enforceability, supporting the assessment of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any dispute relating to this agreement the prevailing party will be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any dispute relating to this agreement, each party will bear their own attorneys' fees and costs, regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the prevailing party's right to recover attorneys' fees, which could conflict with Florida law if a statute provides for fee recovery in certain types of disputes related to confidential information or trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 688.004(1) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) allows a court to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in specific instances. The modification that each party bears their own fees contradicts the possibility of fee shifting to a prevailing party as permitted under the Florida UTSA.

F.S. 688.004 688.004 Damages. — (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (2) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1). History. — s. 4, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts Florida Statute § 688.004(1), which allows a court to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in trade secret misappropriation cases. The modification eliminates the prevailing party's right to recover attorneys' fees, regardless of who prevails. This conflicts with the possibility of fee shifting to a prevailing party as permitted under the Florida UTSA, as described in the law explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms this statute, explicitly stating that a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. The changed text removes this possibility, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Except as permitted above, neither of us will use nor disclose the other's c onfidential information for three years after we receive it. The three - year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as permitted above, neither of us will use nor disclose the other's confidential information for one year after we receive it. The one - year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Florida law, as the reasonable duration depends on the nature of the information and industry standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 542.335(1)(e) specifies that, in the context of trade secrets, a restriction lasting for five years or less is presumed reasonable, but a restriction longer than five years is presumed unreasonable. The reduction to one year could be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect trade secrets, depending on the specific context.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, potentially undermining trade secret protection under Florida law. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute § 542.335(1)(e) considers restrictions of five years or less as presumptively reasonable for trade secrets. Reducing the period to one year could be deemed unreasonable, thus contradicting the legal standard for trade secret protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the Law Explanation, Perturbation Explanation, and the changed text itself, in comparison to the original text, sufficiently indicate a potential legal contradiction. The changed text creates an inconsistency with established legal standards for trade secret protection duration.

perturbation.original_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reaso n by providing the other with 60 days' advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 14 days' advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 14 days could be seen as unreasonably short and potentially conflicting with implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under Florida contract law, depending on the nature of the business relationship and potential harm caused by abrupt termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.304 states that 'Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance or enforcement.' While this statute primarily relates to the Uniform Commercial Code, the principle of good faith applies broadly to contract law in Florida. Reducing the notice period to 14 days may, in certain circumstances, be interpreted as a breach of the duty of good faith, particularly if it deprives the other party of a reasonable opportunity to make alternative arrangements.

F.S. 671.304 671.304 Laws not repealed; precedence where code provisions in conflict with other laws; certain statutory remedies retained. — (1) The article on documents of title (Art. 7) does not repeal or modify any laws prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees’ businesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein; but the fact that such laws are violated does not affect the status of a document of title which otherwise complies with the definition of a “document of title” (s. 671.201 ). (2) The following laws and parts of laws are specifically not repealed and shall take precedence over any provisions of this code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 517 — Sale of securities. (b) Chapter 687 — Interest and usury; lending practices. (c) Chapter 516 — Florida Consumer Finance Act. (d) Chapter 520 — Retail installment sales (Part I, Motor Vehicle Sales Finance Act; Part II, Retail Installment Sales Act; Part III, Installment Sales Finance Act). (e) Chapter 665 — Florida Savings Association Act. (f) Chapter 657 — Credit unions. (g) Chapters 319 and 328 — Title certificates (motor vehicle; vessel). (h) Chapter 538 — Secondhand Dealers and Secondary Metals Recyclers. (i) Chapters 658, 660, and 663. (3) The following laws or parts of laws, although not repealed, shall yield to and be superseded by any provisions of the code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 697 — Instruments deemed mortgages and the nature of a mortgage. (b) Chapter 701 — Assignment and cancellation of mortgages. (c) Chapter 702 — Foreclosure of mortgages. (d) Chapter 727 — General assignments. (4) Notwithstanding any provisions to the contrary in any of the following Florida Statutes, the remedies provided by such statutes shall not restrict the remedies otherwise available to a secured party under this code, but all such remedies shall be cumulatively
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for termination from 60 to 14 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this shorter period may breach the duty of good faith, particularly if it deprives the other party of a reasonable opportunity to make alternative arrangements. Florida Statute § 671.304, as cited in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance or enforcement.' The shorter notice period could be viewed as an inconsistency that undermines this obligation, making the contract potentially unenforceable under Florida law.
